name: General Mathematics
examGuidance: General Mathematics questions should be set in practical, real-world contexts. Use VCE command verbs (calculate, solve, interpret, justify) and apply questions to everyday financial, statistical, geometric and network situations. For technology-active items, assume access to CAS or approved calculator with numerical, graphical, financial and statistical functionality. For Units 1 and 2, all assessment is school-based; for Units 3 and 4, align tasks to the VCAA examination formats. Round answers to the number of decimal places or significant figures specified (typically 2 decimal places for money). Always define variables and state units in solutions. For statistical questions, emphasize the difference between correlation and causation.
out_of_scope: Non-standard probability distributions beyond the Normal model

subtopics.name: Types of Data and Frequency Tables

technique_notes.core_concepts: Classify data as categorical (nominal, ordinal) or numerical (discrete, continuous). Construct frequency and relative frequency tables.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Ensure categories in ordinal data are presented in a logical order. Relative frequencies should sum to 1 or 100%.
anti_prompts: Do not confuse ordinal categorical data with discrete numerical data (e.g., Likert scales).
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Manually tally small datasets and calculate percentages.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Use CAS to generate frequency distributions from raw lists.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Construct and interpret bar charts and pie charts. Use segmented bar charts for bivariate categorical data.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Always include labels and scales. In segmented bar charts, ensure the total for each bar is 100% when comparing groups. Include `chartData` for bar charts.
anti_prompts: Do not use histograms for categorical data.
Avoid using bar charts for numerical data unless it is binned into few categories.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Identify modes and proportions from simple charts.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Generate side-by-side or segmented bar charts to compare groups.

subtopics.name: Dot Plots, Stem Plots and Histograms

technique_notes.core_concepts: Displays for numerical data. Stem plots must have a key. Histograms for large datasets (frequency vs density).
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Describe shape (skewness, symmetry), center (median), and spread. Identify gaps and clusters. Include `chartData` for histograms.
anti_prompts: Do not forget the key in a stem plot.
Ensure histogram bins are equal width.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Construct stem plots by hand and identify the median position.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Adjust bin widths on CAS to investigate different distribution views.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Measures of center. Mean for symmetric data; median for skewed data or data with outliers.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Calculate the median position as $(n+1)/2$. Understand the effect of shifting or scaling data on these statistics.
anti_prompts: Do not calculate the mean if the data is highly skewed or contains outliers without justification.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Calculate mean and median for small, sorted datasets.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Use CAS 1-variable stats functionality for large lists.

subtopics.name: Measures of Spread: Range, IQR and Standard Deviation

technique_notes.core_concepts: Interquartile Range (IQR = $Q_3 - Q_1$). Standard deviation for symmetric distributions.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: IQR is a resistant measure of spread. Range is sensitive to outliers.
anti_prompts: Do not use standard deviation for skewed data if the median is the center measure.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Find IQR from a sorted list of 10-15 values.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Calculate population vs sample standard deviation as specified (usually sample $s_x$).

subtopics.name: Outliers and the 1.5 x IQR Rule

technique_notes.core_concepts: Identify outliers using lower fence ($Q_1 - 1.5 \times \text{IQR}$) and upper fence ($Q_3 + 1.5 \times \text{IQR}$).
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Always show the fence calculations in written responses.
anti_prompts: Do not guess outliers by looking at a plot; always use the rule.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Determine if a specific value is an outlier given $Q_1$ and $Q_3$.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Identify outliers from boxplots generated on CAS.

complexity_levers.easy: Identify outliers given a boxplot with marked dots.
complexity_levers.hard: Calculate fences and identify outliers from a raw dataset.
complexity_levers.extreme: Analyze the impact of removing an outlier on the mean vs the median.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Five-number summary: Min, $Q_1$, Median, $Q_3$, Max. Boxplots with whiskers ending at fences (excluding outliers).
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Label the axis. Show outliers as distinct points ($*$ or $\bullet$). Include `chartData` for boxplots.
anti_prompts: Do not extend whiskers to outliers.
Ensure the box is centered on the median correctly.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Sketch a boxplot from a provided five-number summary.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Generate and compare multiple boxplots on the same axis.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Compare center (median), spread (IQR/range), and shape. Use back-to-back stem plots or parallel boxplots.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Use comparative language (e.g., 'The median for Group A is higher than Group B'). Reference specific values.
anti_prompts: Do not list statistics for each group separately without making a comparison.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Compare two groups using a back-to-back stem plot.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Analyze and interpret parallel boxplots for three or more groups.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Common difference $d$. Recurrence relation $u_{n+1} = u_n + d$. Explicit rule $u_n = u_0 + nd$.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Identify growth (positive $d$) vs decay (negative $d$). Relate to simple interest.
anti_prompts: Do not confuse the index $n$ with the term number (is the first term $u_0$ or $u_1$?).
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Generate the first 5 terms given $u_0$ and $d$.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Find $n$ for a given $u_n$ value using CAS.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Common ratio $R$. Recurrence relation $u_{n+1} = R u_n$. Explicit rule $u_n = u_0 R^n$.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Identify growth ($R > 1$) vs decay ($0 < R < 1$). Relate to compound interest.
anti_prompts: Do not use negative ratios for growth/decay models.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Generate terms for simple ratios like 2 or 0.5.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Investigate long-term behavior (approaching zero vs infinity).

technique_notes.core_concepts: Growth/decay by a constant amount each period based on a percentage of the initial value.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Interest $I =  rac{V_0 	imes r 	imes n}{100}$. Use recurrence relations.
anti_prompts: Do not compound the interest in simple interest problems.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Calculate interest for small number of years/periods.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Compare total interest across different flat-rate investments.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Depreciation based on usage (e.g., per km, per page). $u_{n+1} = u_n - D$.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Identify $D$ as the cost per unit of use. Clearly state the total units of use.
anti_prompts: Do not use time-based depreciation formulas for unit-cost scenarios.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Calculate salvage value after a set usage amount.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Determine the usage level required to reach a specific book value.

subtopics.name: Compound Interest and Reducing Balance Depreciation (Intro)

technique_notes.core_concepts: Growth/decay where the change is a percentage of the *current* value. $u_{n+1} = R u_n$.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Identify $R = 1 + r/100$ for growth, $R = 1 - r/100$ for decay.
anti_prompts: Do not forget to adjust $r$ for the compounding period (e.g., monthly).
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Calculate value after 2 periods by hand.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Use CAS to find the value after many periods ($u_n$).

subtopics.name: Percentage Change, Ratios and the Unitary Method

technique_notes.core_concepts: Calculate markups, discounts, and GST. Use ratios to divide quantities. Use unitary method (find 1 unit) for scaling.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: GST is 10%. To find the pre-GST price, divide the total by 1.1.
anti_prompts: Do not calculate 10% of the GST-inclusive price to find the GST amount.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Calculate 10%, 25%, 50% and simple ratios like 2:1.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Solve complex proportion problems involving multiple steps.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Graph $y=a+bx$. $a$ is the $y$-intercept (vertical intercept), $b$ is the gradient (rate of change).
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Interpret gradient as 'for every unit increase in $x$, $y$ increases/decreases by $b$ units'. Label intercepts clearly.
anti_prompts: Do not swap $a$ and $b$ when interpreting the context.
Ensure units are included for the gradient interpretation.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Sketch a line from its equation and identify intercepts.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Find the equation of a line passing through two specific points.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Translate worded scenarios into $y=a+bx$. Identify explanatory and response variables.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Specify the domain of application (e.g., time $t \ge 0$). Discuss the validity of the model at extreme values.
anti_prompts: Do not extrapolate beyond the context without a disclaimer.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Formulate a simple cost model ($C = I + nR$).
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Fit a linear model to a given set of data points.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Solve $ax+by=c$ and $dx+ey=f$. Intersection represents the point where both conditions are met.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Break-even point: Revenue = Cost ($R=C$). Interpret the point of intersection in context.
anti_prompts: Do not assume a solution exists for parallel lines.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Solve small systems using substitution or elimination.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Use the `solve` or matrix functionality on CAS for larger coefficients.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Graphs with different rules for different domains. Step graphs for tiered pricing.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Use open and closed circles to show endpoint inclusion. Include `chartData` for line segments.
anti_prompts: Do not leave gaps between domain segments in a continuous piecewise model.
Avoid using sloped lines for step graphs (tax, postage).
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Calculate a value from a provided step graph.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Construct the rule for a multi-tiered pricing system.

subtopics.name: Matrix Types, Order and Element Identification

technique_notes.core_concepts: Row, column, square, zero, and identity matrices. Order $m \times n$. Element $a_{ij}$.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Identity matrix $I$ has 1s on the leading diagonal and 0s elsewhere. Order is Rows then Columns.
anti_prompts: Do not confuse order $m \times n$ with $n \times m$ (Columns then Rows is incorrect).
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Identify specific elements in a provided matrix.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Construct matrices to represent network links or data tables.

subtopics.name: Matrix Addition, Subtraction and Scalar Multiplication

technique_notes.core_concepts: Perform operations element-wise. Addition/Subtraction requires identical order.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Scalar multiplication scales every element. Interpret the resulting matrix in context.
anti_prompts: Do not add matrices of different orders.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Perform basic arithmetic on $2 \times 2$ matrices.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Use matrices to combine multiple datasets (e.g., inventory across months).

technique_notes.core_concepts: Multiplication $AB$ is defined if columns of $A$ = rows of $B$. Product order is (rows of $A$) $\times$ (columns of $B$).
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Matrix multiplication is generally non-commutative ($AB \ne BA$).
anti_prompts: Do not attempt to multiply matrices that do not meet the inner dimension condition.
Do not multiply element-wise.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Multiply a row matrix by a column matrix to find a total.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Multiply $3 \times 3$ matrices and interpret the results.

subtopics.name: Determinants, Inverses and Solving Matrix Equations

technique_notes.core_concepts: Determinant $\det(A) = ad-bc$ for $2 \times 2$. Inverse $A^{-1}$. Solve $AX=B$ as $X=A^{-1}B$.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: If $\det(A)=0$, the matrix is singular and has no inverse.
anti_prompts: Do not divide by a matrix; use the inverse.
Ensure you multiply the inverse on the correct side ($A^{-1}B$, not $BA^{-1}$).
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Calculate the determinant of a $2 \times 2$ matrix.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Solve 3-variable systems of linear equations using matrices on CAS.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Communication: links between nodes. Dominance: results in tournaments. $D+D^2$ to rank players.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: A '1' in $D^2$ at $(i, j)$ means $i$ dominates someone who dominates $j$.
anti_prompts: Do not count the leading diagonal in dominance sums unless specifically instructed.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Construct a communication matrix from a small diagram.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Calculate $D+D^2$ for a tournament with 5+ players.

subtopics.name: Intro to Transition Matrices and Equilibrium

technique_notes.core_concepts: Model change between states. $S_{n+1} = T S_n$. Columns of $T$ must sum to 1.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Investigate $S_n$ as $n \to \infty$ to find the equilibrium state.
anti_prompts: Do not put percentages into the transition matrix; use decimal proportions.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Calculate the state after one iteration for two states.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Find the steady-state vector by raising $T$ to a high power (e.g., $T^{50}$).

technique_notes.core_concepts: Explanatory variable ($x$) predicts the response variable ($y$).
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Identify which variable 'explains' the change. In experiments, explanatory is often the manipulated variable.
anti_prompts: Do not swap axes on a scatterplot; $x$ is always the explanatory variable.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Identify $x$ and $y$ from a worded context.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Analyze which variable choice yields a stronger correlation.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Visual display of bivariate data. Describe using direction (+/-), form (linear/non-linear), and strength (weak/moderate/strong).
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Justify the strength based on how closely the points follow a line. Include `chartData` for scatterplots.
anti_prompts: Do not ignore curvature (form) when describing an association.
Avoid using 'strong' for sparse, scattered data.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Describe an association shown in a provided plot.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Construct a scatterplot from a data table on CAS.

subtopics.name: Fitting a Line of Good Fit by Eye

technique_notes.core_concepts: Draw a line that balances points above and below. Find its equation $y=a+bx$.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Ensure the line passes through the centroid $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$ if known.
anti_prompts: Do not connect the first and last points if they are not representative of the trend.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Draw a line of fit by hand and determine its gradient from two points on the line.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Compare a line fit by eye to the least-squares regression line.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Interpolation: prediction within the range of data. Extrapolation: prediction outside the range.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: State that extrapolation is generally less reliable because the trend may not continue.
anti_prompts: Do not treat extrapolation results as certainties.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Use a graph or equation to predict a value within the data range.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Identify the data range on CAS before making predictions.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Association does not imply that one variable *causes* the other. Consider lurking variables.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Use words like 'associated' or 'correlated' instead of 'causes' or 'results in'.
anti_prompts: Do not conclude a causal link from a high correlation coefficient alone.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Discuss potential lurking variables in a given scenario (e.g., ice cream sales and shark attacks).
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Evaluate research claims based on correlation data.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Network terminology. Degree of a vertex is the number of edges connected. Sum of degrees = $2 \times \text{edges}$.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: A loop counts as 2 degrees for that vertex.
anti_prompts: Do not miscount loops when calculating the sum of degrees.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Identify the degree of each vertex in a small graph.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Identify features of large adjacency matrices.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Isomorphic: same structure, different look. Planar: can be drawn without edges crossing. $V-E+F=2$.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Verify isomorphism by comparing vertex degrees and connections. Euler's formula only applies to connected planar graphs.
anti_prompts: Do not apply Euler's formula to non-planar or disconnected graphs.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Redraw a crossing-edge graph in planar form.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Check isomorphism for graphs with 10+ vertices.

subtopics.name: Walks, Trails, Paths, Cycles and Circuits

technique_notes.core_concepts: Walk: any sequence of edges. Trail: no edges repeated. Path: no vertices repeated. Cycle: path ending at start. Circuit: trail ending at start.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Distinguish between these based on the repetition rules for vertices and edges.
anti_prompts: Do not use 'path' and 'trail' interchangeably.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Identify a specific path or cycle in a provided network.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Analyze connectivity in large social networks.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Finding the route with the minimum weight between two vertices.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Systematically list and compare path weights for small networks.
anti_prompts: Do not overlook indirect paths that might have a lower total weight.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Find the shortest path in a 5-6 node weighted graph.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: N/A

technique_notes.core_concepts: A tree connecting all vertices with minimum total edge weight. Prim's: start at a vertex and add the cheapest connected edge.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Ensure the final result is a tree (no cycles).
anti_prompts: Do not form a cycle when adding edges.
Do not miss any vertices.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Apply Prim's algorithm step-by-step and show the order of selection.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Calculate the total cost of a MST for a large utility network.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Relationship $y=kx$ or $y=kx^n$. Graph is a straight line through the origin (for $n=1$).
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Calculate the constant of variation $k$ by substituting a known point. $y \propto x$.
anti_prompts: Do not forget the origin in a direct linear variation sketch.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Find $k$ given $x$ and $y$; use the model to find a third value.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Model real-world proportional relationships (e.g., mass and volume).

technique_notes.core_concepts: Relationship $y=k/x$ or $y=k/x^n$. As $x$ increases, $y$ decreases.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Graph has asymptotes (axes). $y \propto 1/x$.
anti_prompts: Do not cross the axes in an inverse variation sketch.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Identify the shape of an inverse variation curve.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Model relationships like pressure and volume (Boyle's Law).

technique_notes.core_concepts: Transforming $x$ or $y$ to make a non-linear relationship appear linear. Transformations: $x^2$, $1/x$, $\log_{10}(x)$, $\log_{10}(y)$.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Use a residual plot to check if the transformation successfully linearised the data.
anti_prompts: Do not use a transformation that makes the data more curved.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Calculate transformed values for a small set of $(x, y)$ coordinates.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Perform least-squares regression on transformed data on CAS.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Representing values that span several orders of magnitude. A value of $3$ on a log scale represents $10^3 = 1000$.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Interpret the log scale carefully. Each unit increase is a tenfold increase in the original value.
anti_prompts: Do not treat log scale values as linear increases (e.g., 2 is not double 1; it is 10 times larger).
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Convert between raw values and log-scale values (e.g., $\text{pH}$, Richter scale).
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Plot data on a semi-log graph.

subtopics.name: Perimeter and Area of Composite Shapes

technique_notes.core_concepts: Area of circles, triangles, quadrilaterals. Perimeter of sectors and composite figures.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Show the decomposition of the shape into simpler standard shapes.
anti_prompts: Do not forget the straight edges when calculating the perimeter of a semi-circle.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Calculate area and perimeter of shapes made of rectangles and triangles.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Solve land area problems from surveyors' diagrams.

subtopics.name: Volume and Surface Area of Solids

technique_notes.core_concepts: Prisms, pyramids, cylinders, cones, and spheres. $V = \text{Area of base} \times \text{height}$ for prisms.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Distinguish between total surface area and curved surface area.
anti_prompts: Ensure units are cubed for volume and squared for area.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Calculate volume of simple cylinders and prisms.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Solve for an unknown dimension (e.g., radius) given the volume.

technique_notes.core_concepts: $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$. Find the diagonal of a box (space diagonal).
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: In 3D, apply Pythagoras twice or use $d^2 = l^2 + w^2 + h^2$.
anti_prompts: Do not assume a triangle is right-angled without evidence.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Find the hypotenuse of a $(3, 4, 5)$ or $(5, 12, 13)$ triangle.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Calculate heights of pyramids using the slant edge.

subtopics.name: Sine and Cosine Rules (Non-right angled triangles)

technique_notes.core_concepts: Sine Rule: $\frac{a}{\sin A} = \frac{b}{\sin B}$. Cosine Rule: $c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cos C$.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Use Cosine rule when 3 sides are known, or 2 sides and the included angle. Use Sine rule otherwise.
anti_prompts: Be aware of the ambiguous case for the Sine rule (Acute vs Obtuse angle).
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Use exact values for $\sin(30^\circ), \sin(45^\circ), \sin(60^\circ)$.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Solve for all unknown sides and angles of any triangle.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Bearings measured clockwise from North ($000^\circ$ to $360^\circ$).
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Draw a North arrow at every point. Alternate interior angles are equal for parallel North lines.
anti_prompts: Do not confuse bearings with compass directions (e.g., $N45E$ vs $045^\circ$).
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Calculate the back bearing (add/subtract $180^\circ$).
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Solve complex navigation problems using the Sine and Cosine rules.

subtopics.name: The Normal Distribution and the 68-95-99.7% Rule

technique_notes.core_concepts: Properties of the bell-shaped curve. 68% within $1\sigma$, 95% within $2\sigma$, 99.7% within $3\sigma$.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Use the symmetry of the curve. 'Between' means sum the relevant sections. 'Greater than $\mu + \sigma$' is $(100-68)/2 = 16\%$.
anti_prompts: Do not use these percentages for non-normal distributions.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Calculate percentages for exact SD boundaries ($1, 2, 3$).
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Find probabilities for any $x$ value using `normCDF`.

technique_notes.core_concepts: $z = (x - \mu) / \sigma$. Indicates how many standard deviations a value is from the mean.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Positive $z$ is above the mean; negative $z$ is below. Use $z$-scores to compare performance across different tests.
anti_prompts: Do not forget the negative sign for values below the mean.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Calculate $z$ given $x, \mu, \sigma$ or vice versa.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Find the percentage of data above/below a specific $z$-score.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Parameters ($\mu, \sigma$) describe the whole population; statistics ($\bar{x}, s$) describe a sample.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Understand that sample statistics are used to estimate population parameters. Discuss potential sampling bias.
anti_prompts: Do not use $\mu$ and $\sigma$ when referring to a small experimental sample.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Identify the parameter vs the statistic in a worded problem.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: N/A

technique_notes.core_concepts: Line that minimises the sum of the squared residuals. Equation $y = a + bx$.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Interpret $a$ as the predicted value when $x=0$. Interpret $b$ as the average change in $y$ for a 1-unit increase in $x$.
anti_prompts: Do not use $y=mx+c$ notation if the VCAA $y=a+bx$ is specified.
Include units in your interpretation of the slope.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Interpret given coefficients $a$ and $b$ in context.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Calculate $a$ and $b$ using $b = r(s_y/s_x)$ and $a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$ or CAS.

subtopics.name: Correlation Coefficient (r) and Coefficient of Determination (r^2)

technique_notes.core_concepts: $r$ measures strength and direction (-1 to 1). $r^2$ is the proportion of variation in $y$ explained by the variation in $x$.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Interpret $r^2$ as a percentage (e.g., '64% of the variation in weight is explained by the variation in height').
anti_prompts: Do not confuse $r$ (strength) with $b$ (gradient).
Always specify 'variation in [response variable]' in $r^2$ interpretations.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: State the strength and direction given an $r$ value.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Calculate $r$ from a dataset on CAS.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Residual = Actual $y$ - Predicted $y$. Residual plot shows residuals vs $x$.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Random scatter = linear model is appropriate. Pattern (e.g., curve) = non-linear model may be better.
anti_prompts: Do not assume a high $r$ value alone means a linear model is appropriate; always check the residuals.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Calculate a single residual given an actual point and the regression equation.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Generate and interpret a residual plot on CAS.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Applying $x^2, 1/x, \log(x)$ or $y^2, 1/y, \log(y)$ to linearise a curved scatterplot.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Check the 'circle of transformations' to choose the right one based on the curve direction.
anti_prompts: Remember to transform back when making predictions (e.g., if you used $\log y$, use $10^{\text{result}}$ to find $y$).
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Identify which transformation might work for a given curve shape.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Fit a regression line to transformed data and use it for prediction.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Data collected over time. Identify: Trend (long-term up/down), Seasonality (repeating pattern within a year), Cycles (longer term patterns), Irregularity.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Include `chartData` for time series. Describe the overall direction and any obvious peaks/troughs.
anti_prompts: Do not confuse seasonality with cycles.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Qualitatively describe the features of a time series plot.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Plot time series and identify outliers.

subtopics.name: Smoothing Time Series (Moving Means and Medians)

technique_notes.core_concepts: Reducing irregular fluctuations to see the trend. Moving means for general smoothing; moving medians are resistant to outliers.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Centring is required for an even number of points (e.g., 4-month moving mean).
anti_prompts: Ensure you align the smoothed value with the correct time point (the middle of the group).
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Calculate a 3-point moving mean or median.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Apply 12-month smoothing to yearly data on CAS.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Seasonal Index (SI) = Value / Average for year. Sum of SIs = number of seasons. Deseasonalised = Actual / SI.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Interpret SI: SI = 1.2 means '20% above the yearly average'. SI = 0.8 means '20% below'.
anti_prompts: Remember to RE-seasonalise for final forecasts ($Predicted = Deseasonalised \times SI$).
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Calculate a seasonal index from seasonal averages.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Deseasonalise a multi-year time series and find the trend line.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Using a trend line fit to deseasonalised data to predict future values.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: 1. Forecast deseasonalised value using trend line. 2. Multiply by the correct Seasonal Index for that period.
anti_prompts: Do not forget the SI step when predicting for a specific season.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Follow the two-step forecasting process manually.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Evaluate the accuracy of a forecast using historical data.

technique_notes.core_concepts: $V_{n+1} = V_n + d$ (interest) or $V_{n+1} = V_n - d$ (depreciation).
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: $d = (V_0 \times r)/100$ for flat rate. Always show the recurrence relation including $V_0$.
anti_prompts: Do not increase the depreciation amount over time; it is constant.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: State the value after $n$ years using the explicit rule.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: N/A

technique_notes.core_concepts: $V_{n+1} = V_n - D$. Relate to usage rather than time.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Interpret $D$ as the depreciation per unit. $n$ is the number of units used.
anti_prompts: Ensure units match (e.g., if $D$ is per km, $n$ must be km).
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Determine the value after a specific amount of use.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: N/A

technique_notes.core_concepts: $V_{n+1} = R V_n$. $R = 1 + (r/p)/100$ where $p$ is compounding frequency.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Recurrence relation must reflect the compounding period (e.g., $V_{n+1} = 1.005 V_n$ for 6% p.a. monthly).
anti_prompts: Do not use the annual rate $r$ directly if it compounds more frequently.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Calculate the value after 2-3 months by hand.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Find the future value after many years using the explicit rule $V_n = V_0 R^n$.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Nominal rate: stated annual rate. Effective rate: actual annual growth after compounding. $r_{eff} = [(1 + (r/p)/100)^p - 1] \times 100$.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Effective rate is always higher than or equal to the nominal rate. Higher compounding frequency increases the effective rate.
anti_prompts: Do not use simple interest to compare compounding loans.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Qualitatively state which compounding frequency is better for an investor.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Calculate the exact effective rate to choose between two bank offers.

subtopics.name: Reducing Balance Loans and Amortisation Tables

technique_notes.core_concepts: $V_{n+1} = R V_n - d$. $d$ is the regular payment. Table tracks Interest, Principal Reduction, and Balance.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Interest Paid = Current Balance $\times (r/p)/100$. Principal Reduction = Payment - Interest Paid.
anti_prompts: The interest for the current period is calculated on the *previous* balance.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Fill in missing rows of an amortisation table.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Analyze how changing the payment $d$ affects the loan duration.

subtopics.name: Financial Solver for Reducing Balance Loans

technique_notes.core_concepts: N (periods), I% (annual), PV (loan amount, +), PMT (payment, -), FV (future/salvage, -), P/Y, C/Y.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Sign convention: Money you receive is + (PV). Money you pay is - (PMT, FV).
anti_prompts: Do not forget to set FV to 0 if the loan is fully repaid.
Ensure P/Y and C/Y match the payment frequency.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: N/A
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Calculate the time to repay a loan or the remaining balance after 5 years.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Annuity: Investment with regular withdrawals. Perpetuity: Withdrawal equals the interest earned, so the balance never changes.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: For Perpetuity: Payment $d = (V_0 \times r_{period})/100$.
anti_prompts: In a perpetuity, the FV is always exactly $-PV$ (if using CAS) or the same as $V_0$.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Calculate the required deposit for a specific perpetuity monthly income.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Use Financial Solver to find how long an annuity will last.

technique_notes.core_concepts: $V_{n+1} = R V_n + d$. Used for superannuation or savings goals.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: PV is negative (money you put in), PMT is negative (extra savings), FV will be positive (money you get back).
anti_prompts: Ensure the sign of $d$ matches the cash flow direction.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Calculate the value after 2 periods by hand.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Determine the monthly deposit needed to reach a target savings amount.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Order, element identification ($a_{ij}$), powers ($A^n$), determinant, and inverse.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Verify order compatibility before adding or multiplying. Use $I$ for identity properties ($AI=IA=A$).
anti_prompts: Do not use matrix division.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Calculate $2 \times 2$ determinants and inverses.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Find large powers of square matrices.

subtopics.name: Matrix Equations and Systems of Linear Equations

technique_notes.core_concepts: Solving $AX=B$ where $X = A^{-1}B$. Representing word problems as matrix equations.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Clearly define the variable matrix $X$. Ensure the order of $A^{-1}B$ is correct for multiplication.
anti_prompts: Do not use the inverse method if the determinant is zero (lines are parallel).
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Solve 2-variable systems by hand using the inverse matrix.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Solve 3x3 or 4x4 systems on CAS.

technique_notes.core_concepts: $S_{n+1} = T S_n$ and $S_n = T^n S_0$. State vectors $S_n$.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Each column of $T$ must sum to 1. $T_{ij}$ is the probability of moving *from* state $j$ *to* state $i$.
anti_prompts: Do not swap rows and columns in the transition matrix definition.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Calculate $S_1$ given $T$ and $S_0$.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Find the population distribution after 10 time periods.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Steady-state where $T S_{steady} = S_{steady}$. Found by calculating $T^n$ for large $n$.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Equilibrium does not depend on the initial state $S_0$ for regular transition matrices.
anti_prompts: Do not assume every matrix reaches equilibrium (check for regularity).
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: N/A
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Calculate the steady-state percentages for a market share model.

technique_notes.core_concepts: $S_{n+1} = T S_n + B$ where $B$ is a constant column matrix representing additions/removals.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Used for population models with immigration/emigration or restocking.
anti_prompts: Do not forget to add $B$ at each iteration.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Calculate $S_1$ and $S_2$ manually.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Determine if a population will stabilize or crash over time.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Special transition matrix for age-structured populations. Top row: birth rates. Sub-diagonal: survival rates.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Interpret the elements $f_i$ (fecundity) and $s_i$ (survival probability).
anti_prompts: Do not put survival rates on the main diagonal.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Calculate the number of newborns in the next generation.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Analyze the growth rate of a population over several generations.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Trail/Circuit that uses every *edge* exactly once. Circuit starts and ends at the same vertex.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Eulerian Circuit: all vertices have even degree. Eulerian Trail: exactly two vertices have odd degree (starts at one, ends at the other).
anti_prompts: Do not mistake Hamiltonian (vertices) for Eulerian (edges).
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Identify if a network has an Eulerian trail/circuit based on degrees.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: N/A

technique_notes.core_concepts: Path/Cycle that visits every *vertex* exactly once. Cycle ends at start.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: No simple degree-based rule like Euler. Found by inspection.
anti_prompts: Do not use every edge; just every vertex.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Identify a Hamiltonian cycle in a provided graph.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Solve the Traveling Salesman Problem for small networks.

subtopics.name: Flow Networks and the Maximum-Flow Minimum-Cut Theorem

technique_notes.core_concepts: Edges have capacities. Max flow from source to sink. Minimum Cut: a cut that separates source from sink with minimum capacity.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Max Flow = Capacity of the Minimum Cut. Only sum edges crossing from source side to sink side.
anti_prompts: Do not count edges that cross back from sink side to source side in your cut capacity.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Find the capacity of a specific cut and identify the minimum cut.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Determine the bottleneck in a complex pipeline system.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Step-by-step algorithm to find the shortest path from a start node to all other nodes.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Show the temporary labels at each vertex. Permanent labels indicate the shortest distance.
anti_prompts: Do not stop the algorithm until the target vertex is reached and its label is permanent.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Apply Dijkstra's manually to a network of 6-8 nodes.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: N/A

technique_notes.core_concepts: Vertices partitioned into two sets. Edges only connect different sets. Used for tasks and people.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Draw a bipartite graph to show all possible assignments.
anti_prompts: Do not connect vertices within the same set.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Solve a simple 1-to-1 matching problem by inspection.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Analyze optimal task allocation.

subtopics.name: The Hungarian Algorithm for Optimal Matching

technique_notes.core_concepts: Steps: 1. Row reduction. 2. Column reduction. 3. Cover zeros with min lines. 4. Adjust if lines < $n$.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: For maximisation problems, subtract all elements from the largest element first.
anti_prompts: Do not forget to do column reduction after row reduction.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Apply the algorithm to a $3 \times 3$ or $4 \times 4$ cost matrix.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: N/A

technique_notes.core_concepts: Activities as edges (AoE). Nodes represent milestones. Precedence table shows which tasks must finish first.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Use dummy activities (dashed edges) to maintain precedence and uniqueness of edges between nodes.
anti_prompts: Do not use two edges between the same two nodes; use a dummy.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Construct an AoE network from a small precedence table.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: N/A

subtopics.name: Forward and Backward Scanning (EST and LST)

technique_notes.core_concepts: EST (Earliest Start Time) from start node using max path. LST (Latest Start Time) from end node using min path.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Forward scan: take the largest value at merging nodes. Backward scan: take the smallest value.
anti_prompts: Do not confuse EST with EFT (Earliest Finish Time).
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Perform scans and label each node with [EST, LST].
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: N/A

subtopics.name: Float Time and the Critical Path

technique_notes.core_concepts: Float = $LST - EST$. Critical path: sequence of tasks with zero float. Minimum completion time = length of critical path.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: A project can have more than one critical path.
anti_prompts: Do not assume all paths with same length as critical path are critical if they have float.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Identify the critical path and calculate float times for non-critical activities.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Analyze how delays in specific tasks affect the project deadline.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Reducing activity times to shorten the project at a cost.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Only crash activities on the critical path. Be aware that the critical path may change after crashing.
anti_prompts: Do not crash a task if it is not on the *current* critical path; it won't reduce project time.
technique_notes.tech_free_rules: Determine the minimum project time after crashing 1-2 specific activities.
technique_notes.tech_active_rules: Find the most cost-effective crashing strategy to meet a deadline.
